section.title: Our Great High Priest
section.sectionRationale: These three verses are the hinge of the letter, gathering the high priest theme introduced in chapter 2 and launching the sustained argument of chapters 5 to 10. Because Jesus is both exalted through the heavens and able to sympathize with our weakness, believers are called to hold fast and to draw near boldly to the throne of grace.
section.thesis: Jesus, the Son of God, is our great high priest who has passed through the heavens and yet was tempted in every way as we are, without sin; therefore we should hold firmly to our confession and approach the throne of grace with confidence, to receive mercy and timely grace.
section.frameNote: Two calls flowing from one great fact: because our high priest is both exalted and sympathetic, first hold firmly to what we profess, and then draw near with confidence to the throne of grace.

units.label: Hold fast: our exalted, sympathetic priest
units.note: tempted as we are, yet without sin

verses.text: Therefore, since we have a great {{archiereus|high priest}} who has passed through the heavens, Jesus the Son of God, let us hold firmly to what we profess.
For we do not have a high priest who is unable to {{sympatheo|sympathize}} with our weaknesses, but we have one who was tempted in every way that we are, yet was without sin.

units.label: Draw near to the throne of grace

verses.text: Let us then approach the throne of grace with {{parresia|confidence}}, so that we may receive mercy and find {{charis|grace}} to help us in our time of need.

archiereus.gloss: High priest, the supreme mediator between God and the people. Jesus is the great high priest who has passed through the heavens into God's presence, uniting exaltation and nearness in a way no earthly priest ever could.

sympatheo.gloss: To suffer with, to feel alongside, to sympathize. Jesus is not a remote priest; having been tempted in every way, He feels with our weaknesses from the inside, so His help is that of one who truly understands.

parresia.gloss: Boldness, confidence, freedom of speech. Because of our high priest, we may come to God's throne not cowering but with confident freedom, speaking plainly as welcomed children rather than fearful strangers.

charis.gloss: Grace, undeserved favour and help. The throne we approach is a throne of grace, from which we receive mercy for our failures and grace precisely timed to help us in our moment of need.

basic.context: The writer has just described the word of God that exposes every heart. That could leave us afraid to come near God. But now comes the answer: we have a great high priest, Jesus, who has passed into heaven itself and yet knows our weakness from the inside, having been tempted as we are, without ever sinning. So we can come boldly to God's throne, which is a throne of grace, and find mercy and help.
basic.hermeneutics: These verses function as a pivot: 'therefore' looks back to the priest theme, and 'let us' twice looks forward to the exhortations that follow. Read them together: the exalted and sympathetic priest grounds both holding fast and drawing near.
basic.originalLanguages: Jesus is our archiereus, high priest, who can sympatheō, sympathize, with our weakness. So we approach the throne with parrēsia, bold confidence, to receive charis, grace, that helps in time of need.
basic.typology: The high priest who once a year passed through the veil into the Most Holy Place foreshadows Jesus, who has passed through the heavens into God's presence. The mercy seat above the ark foreshadows the throne of grace we now approach.
basic.christ: Christ is the great high priest, fully God as the Son who has passed through the heavens, and fully able to sympathize as the man who was tempted in every way yet without sin. He is both exalted and near, and He opens the way to God's throne.
basic.god: God's throne, which might terrify us, is here called a throne of grace. God welcomes His people to come with confidence and dispenses mercy and well-timed grace to those in need.
basic.crossReferences: Hebrews 2 introduced the merciful high priest. Hebrews 10 urges us again to draw near with confidence. Romans 5 speaks of access into grace through Christ.
basic.oneStory: The whole priestly system pointed to a mediator who could bring people to God. In Christ that mediator has come, and the way to God's throne stands open.

context.literary: The three verses form a bracket with 10:19-23, which repeats the same calls to hold fast and to draw near with confidence. They round off the warning of 3:7-4:13 and open the exposition of Christ's priesthood in chapters 5 to 10.
context.historical: Readers who knew the temple system understood that ordinary people could not approach the Most Holy Place; only the high priest entered, once a year, with blood. The claim that all believers may draw near with confidence is a striking announcement of the new access won by Christ.
context.canonical: The passage crystallizes the New Testament theme of confident access to God through Christ, joining Romans 5:1-2 and Ephesians 2:18, and grounds the church's practice of bold, expectant prayer.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To comfort and summon the readers: because their high priest is both exalted and sympathetic, they should hold firmly to their confession and come confidently to God for mercy and help.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: Verse 15 describes the priest; verses 14 and 16 prescribe the response, hold fast and draw near. The enduring command is confident, dependent approach to God through Christ.

debates.question: How could Jesus be 'tempted in every way as we are' and yet be without sin?

views.label: Real temptation fully resisted
views.case: Jesus faced genuine temptation across the range of human experience and never yielded; His sinlessness means He endured the full force of temptation to the end, so He knows its power better, not less, than those who give in.

views.label: Sympathy grounded in shared human experience
views.case: The emphasis falls on His true humanity and solidarity with us; whatever the mode of His temptation, He truly experienced human weakness and pressure, and so can feel with us.

debates.bearing: Either way His sinlessness qualifies rather than distances Him: He is the sympathetic helper precisely because He met temptation and overcame it without sin.

study.originalLanguages: The passage rests on three Greek words that shape Christian prayer. Jesus is the archiereus, the high priest, but a great one who has passed through the heavens, both exalted and enthroned. He is able to sympatheō, to suffer alongside us, to feel our weakness from within, because He was tempted in every way as we are, yet apart from sin. Therefore we come with parrēsia, the bold, outspoken confidence of a welcomed child, to the throne of charis, grace, where God gives mercy for the past and grace for the present, help exactly timed for the hour of need.

typology.type: The high priest passing through the veil on the Day of Atonement
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in Jesus, who has passed through the heavens into the presence of God on our behalf.

typology.type: The mercy seat above the ark
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in the throne of grace, from which mercy and help now flow to all who draw near through Christ.

typology.type: The sympathetic priest able to deal gently with the weak
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in Jesus, tempted as we are yet without sin, who feels with our weakness perfectly.

study.christ: Christ is the great high priest at the heart of these verses, and both His exaltation and His humanity are essential. As the Son of God who has passed through the heavens, He is enthroned in glory; as the one tempted in every way as we are, yet without sin, He is intimately able to sympathize with our weakness. His sinlessness does not make Him distant but qualifies Him to help, for He met temptation at full strength and overcame. Through Him the throne of God becomes a throne of grace to which we may come with confidence.
study.god: God is revealed as the One whose throne is a throne of grace. The same throne that the exposing word of the previous verses might make us dread is here opened as the place where mercy and timely help are freely given. God welcomes His people to approach with confidence rather than fear, and He meets them with exactly the grace their moment of need requires. His holiness has not diminished, but through the high priest it is now a holiness we may draw near to safely.

crossReferences.note: The merciful and faithful high priest, able to help the tempted, introduced here and now applied.
The parallel call to draw near with a true heart in full assurance, by the blood of Jesus.
Through Christ we have obtained access into the grace in which we stand.
Through Him we both have access in one Spirit to the Father, matching the confident approach.

study.oneStory: The long story of priests and sacrifices was always about access, about how sinful people could come near a holy God. In Jesus, the great high priest who is both exalted through the heavens and touched by our weakness, that access is finally and fully secured. The throne of judgment becomes a throne of grace, and the whole biblical drama of drawing near reaches its goal in the invitation to come with confidence and find help.

quiz.q: What two truths about Jesus ground the call to draw near?

options.text: He is exalted through the heavens and yet sympathizes with our weakness.
options.note: Verses 14 to 15 hold together His exaltation and His sympathy.

options.text: He is distant and unfamiliar with human life.
options.note: The opposite: He was tempted as we are and feels with us.

options.text: He is sympathetic but not truly exalted.
options.note: He is both; He has passed through the heavens as the Son of God.

options.text: He is exalted but unable to understand us.
options.note: He is exalted and also fully able to sympathize.

quiz.q: What does it mean that Jesus was tempted 'yet without sin'?

options.text: He faced real temptation at full strength and never yielded.
options.note: His sinlessness means He endured temptation fully, qualifying Him to help.

options.text: He was tempted yet, as the sinless Son, could not finally fail.
options.note: There is truth that He could not ultimately fail, yet the emphasis is that He met real temptation at full strength and truly sympathizes.

options.text: He sinned only in small, forgivable ways.
He was tempted but it did not really affect Him.
options.note: The temptation was genuine; He truly sympathizes with our weakness.

quiz.q: How are we to approach God's throne?

options.text: With confidence, to receive mercy and find grace to help in time of need.
options.note: Verse 16 calls for bold, confident approach.
The throne is a throne of grace; we come with confidence.

options.text: Only through an earthly priest once a year.
options.note: Through Christ all believers may draw near freely.

options.text: Only after we have made ourselves worthy.
options.note: We come for mercy and help, not because we have made ourselves worthy.

quiz.q: What is the throne we approach called, and why does it matter?

options.text: A throne of grace, so we find mercy and help rather than condemnation.
options.note: God's throne is grace toward those who come through Christ.

options.text: A throne of terror to keep us away.
options.note: It might have terrified us, but through Christ it is a throne of grace.

options.text: A throne of pure law with no mercy.
options.note: It is explicitly a throne of grace giving mercy.

options.text: A throne reserved only for angels.
options.note: It is open to believers who draw near through their high priest.

questions: Jesus sympathizes with your weakness from the inside. How does that change what you feel able to bring to Him?
The throne of God is a throne of grace. Do you approach God more as a throne of grace or a throne of fear, and why?
We are invited to come with confidence. What holds you back from praying boldly and honestly?
Grace is promised to help 'in time of need'. Where do you need God's timely help right now, and will you ask?
Because our priest is both exalted and near, we are told to hold firmly to what we profess. What strengthens your grip on your confession when it is tested?

translationNotes.note: 'Passed through the heavens' pictures Jesus entering the very presence of God, surpassing the high priest who merely passed through the veil into the earthly Most Holy Place.
'Sympathize' translates a verb meaning to suffer or feel with another; it denotes fellow feeling grounded in shared experience, not mere pity from a distance.
'With confidence' renders parrēsia, boldness or freedom of speech; the same freedom of access is repeated in 10:19, framing the priestly section.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Hebrews 4:14-16. No em dashes occurred in this passage. Sermon set to none; no expositor series confirmed this session.
